agent_configs.system_prompt: You are in an open-ended conversation. Each reply must introduce a genuinely new idea, fact, or question -- never repeat points already made or keep circling the same topic.

injection.text: Sourdough baking rewards patience above all else. A wild-yeast starter is a living culture of flour and water that you feed daily until it doubles reliably, smelling of ripe apples and yogurt. When you finally mix the dough, long cold fermentation in the refrigerator develops flavour and strengthens the gluten, so the crumb opens into glossy, irregular holes and the crust shatters when you tear it.

injections.text: Sourdough baking rewards patience above all else. A wild-yeast starter is a living culture of flour and water that you feed daily until it doubles reliably, smelling of ripe apples and yogurt. When you finally mix the dough, long cold fermentation in the refrigerator develops flavour and strengthens the gluten, so the crumb opens into glossy, irregular holes and the crust shatters when you tear it.

Coral reefs are built by tiny animals called polyps that secrete limestone skeletons over thousands of years. Each polyp hosts symbiotic algae that photosynthesize and give the reef its vivid colour. Mordants like alum bind the colour to the fibre so it survives washing, and the same dye bath can give a dozen shades depending on the metal in the pot and the mineral content of the water. The tea ceremony in Japan reduces hospitality to its quietest essence, every gesture rehearsed until it looks effortless. The host wipes the bowl, whisks the powdered green tea to a jade foam, and turns it so the guest drinks from the fairest side. When that rain finally comes, a valley can bloom overnight into a carpet of wildflowers, and the whole frantic cycle of pollination and seeding races to finish before the heat returns and the soil cracks dry again.

Chess spread along trade routes from India through Persia to Europe, and the pieces changed as they travelled. The counsellor beside the king became a queen, gaining the sweeping power that makes the modern game so sharp. Glaciers are rivers of ice that flow downhill at the pace of fingernails growing, grinding mountains into powder and carving valleys shaped like the letter U. The blue in deep crevasses is ancient compressed air. A honeybee colony is a single organism spread across forty thousand bodies. Workers dance in figure eights on the comb, angling their waggle toward the sun to tell sisters exactly where the flowers bloom. Sourdough baking rewards patience above all else. A wild-yeast starter is a living culture of flour and water that you feed daily until it doubles reliably, smelling of ripe apples and yogurt. Above five thousand metres the air holds half the oxygen it does at sea level, and the body begins to consume its own muscle to survive. Climbers move slowly, breathing four times for every step. Good olive oil is fruit juice with a short shelf life, not a pantry staple that lasts forever.

John Coltrane practised scales until his lips bled, chasing a sound that felt like prayer. His quartet turned a Broadway show tune into a spiritual suite that stretched past thirty minutes. A fresh oil bites at the back of the throat, a peppery sting from antioxidants that fades within a year, which is why the harvest date matters far more than any label promising extra virgin purity. As they retreat they leave behind moraines, long ridges of rubble that mark where the ice once ended, and meltwater lakes that glow a milky turquoise from the rock flour suspended in them. Surveyors sighted along smoking poles to hold a true line for miles.
